coredata.dc:title: A highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting Cr(VI) and cell imaging based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sheng, Liying
Huangfu, Baihe
Xu, Qinhai
Tian, Wenli
Li, Zhenjiang
Meng, Alan
Tan, Shuqin
coredata.dc:description: A one-step hydrothermal process to synthesize N-doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) using PVP K90 as the novel self-passivation agent, in which the self-passivation and N-doping is realized simultaneously, is first proposed. The as-obtained N-GQDs with an average diameter of 1.9 ± 0.6 nm can emit blue fluorescence (365 nm excitation), and possess a high fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 64.2%. Due to the inner filter effect, N-GQDs are considered as outstanding fluorescent probes for detecting Cr(VI), which exhibit a lower detection limit (91 nM) and a wider linear range (0.1–100 μM). Significantly, the fluorescent probe also indicates a remarkably high selectivity towards Cr(VI) among other metal ions with high concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs quenched by Cr(VI) is significantly turned on through adding reducing agent. What’s more, N-GQDs have been successfully used to detect Cr(VI) in running water. In addition, the as-obtained N-GQDs could be further applied in cell imaging due to its biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Therefore, N-GQDs could be a wondrous sensing system for detecting Cr(VI) and fluorescent marker of MCF-7 cells with a wide range of potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Self-passivation agent
Fluorescence probe
Cr(VI) sensing
MCF-7 cancer cells imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-GQDs. (Insert: Particle size distribution histogram of N-GQDs.) (B) HRTEM image of N-GQDs. (Inserts: (I-1) Magnified image of “I” N-GQDs, (I-2) the corresponding selected area FFT image. (II-1) Magnified image of “II” N-GQDs.) (C) Full scan XPS spectrum of N-GQDs. (D) High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s. (E) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s. (F) High-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths (insert: the photographs of N-GQDs solution under the daylight (left, a) and UV light (365 nm, right, b) irradiation.) (B) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of N-GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs at the presence of the different concentrations of Cr(VI) at λex = 360 nm. (B) The relationship between maximum fluorescence intensity and the logarithm (Base 10) of Cr(VI) concentration within the range of −11.3 to −3.3. (C) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm (Base 10) of Cr(VI) concentration in the range of −7 to −4. (D) The recovery fluorescence spectrum of N-GQDs containing Cr(VI). (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs containing different concentrations of Cr(VI) in tap water at λex = 360 nm. (F) The relationship between the maximum fluorescence intensity and the logarithm (Base 10) of Cr(VI) concentration in tap water within the range of −10.3 to −3.3. (Insert: the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm (Base 10) of Cr(VI) concentration in tap water within the range of −7.3 to −3.3.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs at the presence of only water (blank sample I) and metal ions solutions (1 mM) at λex = 360 nm. (B) Photographs of the aqueous N-GQDs solution containing various metal ions under illumination of daylight (up) and UV light (down) at λex = 365 nm. (C) Normalized fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs at the presence of only water (blank sample I) and anions solutions (1 mM) at λex = 360 nm. (D) Normalized fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs by adding water (blank sample II) and metal ions solutions (1 mM) into N-GQDs solution (1/10) containing Cr(VI) at λex = 360 nm. (E) Fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs and N-GQDs containing Cr(VI). (F) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs solution and N-GQDs solution containing Cr(VI).

(A) The bright field image of MCF-7 cells cultured with N-GQDs for 16 h at 37 °C. (B) Laser confocal microscopic image of MCF-7 cancer cells incubated with N-GQDs for 16 h at 37 °C under 405 nm excitation light.
Schematic of N-GQDs for detecting Cr(VI) ion and imaging MCF-7 cancer cells.
Comparison of different probes for detecting Cr(VI).

head.ce:title: A highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting Cr(VI) and cell imaging based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-step hydrothermal process to synthesize N-doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) using PVP K90 as the novel self-passivation agent, in which the self-passivation and N-doping is realized simultaneously, is first proposed. The as-obtained N-GQDs with an average diameter of 1.9 ± 0.6 nm can emit blue fluorescence (365 nm excitation), and possess a high fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 64.2%. Due to the inner filter effect, N-GQDs are considered as outstanding fluorescent probes for detecting Cr(VI), which exhibit a lower detection limit (91 nM) and a wider linear range (0.1–100 μM). Significantly, the fluorescent probe also indicates a remarkably high selectivity towards Cr(VI) among other metal ions with high concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs quenched by Cr(VI) is significantly turned on through adding reducing agent. What’s more, N-GQDs have been successfully used to detect Cr(VI) in running water. In addition, the as-obtained N-GQDs could be further applied in cell imaging due to its biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Therefore, N-GQDs could be a wondrous sensing system for detecting Cr(VI) and fluorescent marker of MCF-7 cells with a wide range of potential applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, water pollution containing heavy metal ions has become a global environmental problem [1]. Due to the severe effects on the environment and human health, heavy metal pollution has received considerable attention [2]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), as one of the most prevalent toxic heavy metal ions, can extremely harm the biosphere, even trace amounts Cr(VI) could cause an irreversible damage to human health [3a,b] [3a,b]. In order to protect people’s health and environment, many countries have restricted the concentration of Cr(VI). The new regulations of US Environmental Protection Agency confirmed Cr(III) was non-toxic, hexavalent chromium should not exceed 2 mM. It clearly defined Cr(VI) must not exceed 1 mM in EU RoHS Directive. The standard of drinking water quality and the standard of surface water environment quality in China specified explicitly that the content of Cr(VI) in water could not outstrip 0.96 μM. The World Health Organization proposes a guideline limit of 0.8 μM for Cr(VI) in drinking water. Hence, it is of great significance to develop highly selective and excellently sensitive probes for the detection of Cr(VI) to protect the health of people and animals. At present, there are many kinds of chemosensors for Cr(VI) detection. Zhang et al. synthesized molybdenum sulfide composite for detecting Cr(VI) in water with the detection range of 0.5–328 μM (China invention patent, No. 201410293029.3). Chen et al. made use of gold nanoparticles for the detection of Cr(VI) with a range of 1 μM–20 μM and the detection limit was 2 μM [4]. Kong et al. used copper nanoclusters to detect Cr(VI). The detection limit was 1.5 μM and the linear range was from 0 μM to 45 μM [3b]3b. Although these chemosensors can be applied to detect Cr(VI), they still suffer from high cost, complex operation, poor sensitivity and selectivity, which have affected the efficiency of Cr(VI) detection.

Giving a broad overview of most nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted more and more attention due to their attractive physical and chemical properties and wide application prospects [5,6]. GQDs exhibited fairly low toxicity, considerably stability, outstanding solubility, better surface grafting, high electrical conductivity and excellent thermal conductivity compared with the conventional semiconductor quantum dots such as CdS and CdTe [5,6]. Compared with 2D graphene sheets, GQDs exhibit fine fluorescence characteristics attribute to quantum confinement and edge effects [7]. Otherwise, compared with CQDs, GQDs have explicit atom-thick lattice and excellent crystalline structure [8], so that the quantum yield and fluorescence intensity of GQDs are greater. Recently, GQDs as fluorescent probe are widely used for measuring metal ions with better detection performance because of its excellent fluorescent properties. Zhu et al. utilized GQDs to detect Fe3+ [9]. Li et al. reported GQDs for detection of Hg2+ [10]. Therefore, GQDs as fluorescent probe are promising materials for the detection of metal ions. Moreover, GQDs possesses excellent fluorescent properties, biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, so it can also be used for cell imaging. Wang et al. fabricated GQDs for HeLa cells labeling [11]. Dong et al. reported GQDs for labeling U251 Glioma cell [12]. Li et al. prepared GQDs and it could be served as probes for live cell staining [13]. Accordingly, GQDs had a broad application prospect in cancer imaging.

To further enhance the QY, fluorescence intensity and application abilities of GQDs, N-doping is used by inducing an upward shift in the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [14,15]. N doped into graphene was highly effective measure to adjust its band gap and creating large number of localized energy levels near the conduction band to achieve a dramatic increase in fluorescence intensity [16,17]. Fang et al. prepared N-GQDs with a high QY (23.1%) by using a facile solvothermal [18]. Ju et al. synthesized N-GQDs with 23.3% QY via hydrothermal treatment of GQDs using hydrazine [19]. Hu et al. fabricated N-GQDs by hydrothermal treatment of GO in the presence of ammonia, and the acquired N-GQDs possessed a maximum QY up to 24.6% [20]. Unfortunately, all of the aforementioned strategies suffer from disadvantages like complicated and tedious processes, multistep reactions, harsh conditions and high cost, limiting their widespread use. Accordingly, it is highly meaningful to exploit an easy-to-control approach and synthesize N-GQDs with high performance for practical applications.

The passivation agent is another way to increase the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield. The passivation process of GQDs is often realized through two steps. GQDs are synthesized in the first step, and then they are passivated with the passivation. Compared with ordinary passivation method, the self-passivation method can make the self-passivating agent as raw material to realize simultaneously the synthesis and fluorescent functionalized of GQDs in a single step [21]. Accordingly, it is of great research values and significance to accomplish the self-passivation and N-doping simultaneously by the one-step hydrothermal method to obtain N-GQDs with high fluorescence intensity and quantum yield for detecting Cr(VI) and labeling MCF-7 cancer cells, which has not been reported yet.

Herein, a novel, economical and environmentally friendly one-step hydrothermal method was exploited to synthesize the water-soluble, nontoxic and stability N-GQDs by using glutamic acid as nitrogen source, citric acid as carbon source, PVP K90 as the novel self-passivation agent and carbon-nitrogen source for the first time, in which the self-passivation and N-doping were able to accomplish simultaneously. The as-obtained N-GQDs displayed excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence performance and a high fluorescent quantum yield. Due to the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of N-GQDs turned off when Cr(VI) was added into N-GQDs. Based on this phenomenon, N-GQDs fluorescent probe could serve as a promising sensing platform for determination of Cr(VI) with a low limit of detection (LOD), a wide linear range, outstanding selectivity, high immunity and excellent sensitivity. What’s more, N-GQDs could also be applied to detect Cr(VI) in running water sample, which showed that N-GQDs had practical application value in the field of Cr(VI) detection. Moreover, N-GQDs were also applied to MCF-7 cancer cells imaging, indicating that N-GQDs had also important applications in the aspects of biomarkers (Scheme 1 ).

Citric acid, Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP K90) and K2Cr2O7 were obtained from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd. l-glutamic acid was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. PVP K30 was obtained from Tianjin BASF Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) 600 was supplied by Shanghai Shanpu Chemical Co., Ltd. PEG 1000 was bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. RPMI Medium Modified (DMEM) was supplied by Hyclone (Logan, Utah). Penicillin Streptomycin (Pen Strep) was obtained from Gibco (USA). Foetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was purchased from Hyclone (Australia). The cell culture dish was obtained from Corning (New York, USA). 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2h-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Standard stock solution (1 mM) of Cr(VI) was prepared by dissolving K2Cr2O7 in distilled water. The rest of solutions (1 mM) were obtained by dissolving FeSO4·7H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2·1/2H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, AgNO3, NiSO4·6H2O, CoSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, KCl, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Hg(NO3)2, KMnO4, Na2SO4, NaCl, Na2SO3, NaAc, NaOH and Na2S2O5 in deionized water, respectively. All the chemicals were AR grade and no further purification was required. MCF-7 cells were purchased from Gaining biological.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were taken on a JEM-2100 TEM (Japan). XPS analysis was conducted on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Setting parameters: monochromatic Al Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV), operated at 150 W, 500 μm beam spot. The FT-IR spectroscopy was recorded by using the Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FT-IR Spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained through HITACHI F-2700 and HITACHI F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometers. The fluorescence lifetime was performed on an Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). UV–Vis spectrum was conducted via a Lambda 35 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Singapore). The fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells were taken on a laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus Fluoview FV 1000, USA).

N-GQDs were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method. Different quantity of PEG 600 (Table S1 in Supplementary Material), PEG 1000 (Table S2 in Supplementary Material), PVP K30 (Table S3 in Supplementary Material), PVP K90 (Table S4 in Supplementary Material) and 0.5 g citric acid and 0.5 g glutamic acid were dissolved in deionized water (30 mL), respectively. The solutions were heated and stirred at 80 °C for 20 min and then were sonicated in distilled water for 10 min. The solutions were transferred into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and reacted for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the products were centrifuged at 14600 rpm for 5 min. Subsequently, the products were filtered and dialyzed for one day in dialysis bags (1000 MWCO). N-GQDs without self-passivation agent prepared under the above conditions were taken as control.

The sensitivity of N-GQDs for Cr(VI) detection was confirmed by adding 0.5 mL K2Cr2O7 solutions with different concentrations Cr(VI) (0.5 mM, 0.1 mM, 50 μM, 10 μM, 5 μM, 1 μM, 0.5 μM, 0.1 μM, 50 nM, 5 nM, 0.5 nM, 50 pM and 5 pM) to N-GQDs solutions, respectively. To estimate the selectivity of N-GQDs towards Cr(VI), the influence of different metal ions (Ag+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn(VII)) (1 mM) and anions solutions (AC−, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−) (1 mM) was investigated under the same conditions. The as-obtained N-GQDs were diluted 10 times with distilled water and marked as N-GQDs (1/10), 0.9 mL distilled water (blank sample I), 0.9 mL metal ions solutions (Ag+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn(VII), Cr(VI)), 0.9 mL anions solutions (AC−, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−) were added to 0.3 mL N-GQDs solution (1/10), respectively. The fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs containing Cr(VI) (1 mM) was measured by adding Na2S2O5 (0.1 mM) and FeSO4 (0.1 mM) into N-GQDs solutions containing Cr(VI), the experimental data under different conditions was displayed in Table S5 (Supplementary Material). The immunity of N-GQDs for detecting Cr(VI) were recorded via adding 1 mL distilled water (blank sample II), 1 mL metal ions solutions (Ag+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn(VII)) into 1 mL K2Cr2O7 solution (10−3 M) and 1 mL N-GQDs solution (1/10), respectively.

To evaluate biotoxicity of the as-obtained N-GQDs towards MCF-7 cells, MTT assay was conducted. Firstly, MCF-7 cells were seeded onto 96-well plates (200 μL, 1 × 106 cells per well) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 atmosphere. Then the media were removed, and the as-obtained N-GQDs with various concentrations were added into each well and incubated for another 24 h. After that, 100 μL 5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added into each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, the remaining MTT solution in each well was thrown out and 150 μL DMSO was added. The cells without N-GQDs but with 20 μL PBS were taken as control. Finally, the absorbance of the solution was acquired at 490 nm via a microplate reader.

The cell viability was obtained using the following equation:
(1)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y ( % ) =
I
t r e a t e d
I
c o n t r o l
Where I treated represents the absorbance with the presence of N-GQDs, I control represents the absorbance without the presence of N-GQDs (control).

MCF-7 cells were cultivated in DMEM supplemented with FBS and Pen Strep at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 environment, and the cells were incubated with N-GQDs in the cell culture dishes for 16 h. Media in the dishes were discarded and the cells were gently rinsed twice with PBS. The fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells were taken on a laser scanning confocal microscope (405 nm excitation).

TEM image in Fig. 1 A displays that the as-obtained N-GQDs are dispersed in aqueous solution evenly. One hundred and twenty-one N-GQDs from TEM are randomly selected for measuring the particle size. It can be concluded from the particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 1A insert that the mean grain size of N-GQDs is 1.9 ± 0.6 nm, which is smaller than the literature reported value [21,22]. Fig. 1B is HRTEM image, (I-1) inserted in Fig. 1B is magnified image of “I” N-GQDs, it can be found that many six-member rings are uniformly arranged in a hexagonal honeycomb carbon atom grid. The well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing distance of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) plane. It can be seen from (I-2) (FFT image) inserted in Fig. 1B that N-GQDs are almost graphene single crystals with few defects. (II-1) inserted in Fig. 1B is the magnified image of “II” N-GQDs. It can be found that N-GQDs possess the twinning structure. The aforementioned results illustrate that N-GQDs have both perfect lattice structure and twinning structure. The AFM results in Fig. S1A (Supplementary Material) indicate N-GQDs are few-layer graphene. Additionally, XRD results in Fig. S1B (Supplementary Material) also manifest N-GQDs have graphene structure. Fig. 1C displays XPS spectrum of N-GQDs. The predominant peaks located at about 284.7, 398.4 and 531.3 eV are attributed to the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s elements in survey spectrum, respectively. This also manifests that N-GQDs are consisted of three elements of C, N and O, which is consistent with EDS results of N-GQDs in Fig. S1C (Supplementary Material). The high-resolution N1s spectrum is plotted in Fig. 1D. From Fig. 1D, the N1s spectrum is best fitted with two peaks at 398.0 and 398.7 eV, which were ascribed to N–H, C–N groups, respectively [23], indicating nitrogen was successfully doped into the lattice of GQDs [24]. The peaks located at 284.6, 284.83 and 285.7 eV are put down to C–C/CC, C–O and C–H/C–N groups [25] in C1s spectrum in Fig. 1E, respectively. From Fig. 1F, the deconvoluted peaks located at 530.7, 531.3 and 532 eV are assigned to O–C, CO and O–H groups [26,27], respectively, in the high-resolution O1s spectrum. XPS characterizations also show that there are lots of functional groups containing nitrogen, oxygen and carbon in N-GQDs, which are largely coincident with the FT-IR results (Fig. S1D in Supplementary Material). Based on the aforementioned results, there are CC, CO, C–N, C–O and other groups in N-GQDs.

Moreover, to further corroborate the intrinsic structure of the as-obtained N-GQDs, Raman spectrum is conducted and displayed in Fig. S1E (Supplementary Material). The remarkable peak appeared at 1351 cm−1 are ascribed to D band correlated with the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, while the peak situated at around 1610 cm−1 corresponded to G band related to the presence of sp3 defects. It can be found that the intensity of D band was stronger than that of G band, confirming graphene structure existed in the as-obtained N-GQDs.

To investigate the influence of the various self-passivation agents on the fluorescence intensity, the different passivation agents (PVP K90, PVP K30, PEG 1000, PEG 600) were selected to synthetize N-GQDs. Under the same measuring condition, the effect of the quantity of the different self-passivation agents on the fluorescence intensity was investigated. The results were shown in Figs. S2A–D (Supplementary Material), which show that 0.7 g PEG 600, 0.6 g PEG 1000, 0.7 g PVP K30, 0.7 g PVP K90 is the optimal quantity, respectively, when the quantity of citric acid and glutamic acid were all 0.5 g. The fluorescence emission spectrum of N-GQDs prepared by using the various kinds of self-passivation agents with the optimal quantity are displays in Fig. S2E (Supplementary Material). Compared with the blank sample (N-GQDs prepared without self-passivation agents), the fluorescence intensities were all increased by using the self-passivation agents (0.7 g PEG 600, 0.6 g PEG 1000, 0.7 g PVP K30, 0.7 g PVP K90). It indicated that the self-passivation agents were beneficial to the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensities were significantly enhanced by using 0.7 g PVP K90 and 0.7 g PVP K30 as self-passivation agents comparing with that of using 0.6 g PEG 1000 and 0.7 g PEG 600 as self-passivation agents. The possible reasons were as follows: PVP contains nitrogen atoms [28], while PEG does not contain nitrogen atoms. PVP is not only a passivation agent but also a nitrogen source. Hence, in addition to passivation effect, PVP can also increase the nitrogen content in N-GQDs during the preparation process [29]. Due to N is incorporated into GQDs π-conjugated system and more active sites are obtained, the electrons are excited from the ground state to the excited state by light, more photons are released when the electrons returned to the ground state, improving the fluorescence performance of N-GQDs [30]. The fluorescence strength was markedly improved by using PVP K90 instead of PVP K30 as self-passivation agents. The possible reason may be that the viscosity coefficient (K value) of PVP was larger, the adhesive property was stronger, which was conducive to enhance the fluorescence strength of N-GQDs [31]. All above, N-GQDs with the optimum fluorescence intensity could be obtained by using 0.5 g citric acid as carbon source, 0.5 g glutamic acid as nitrogen source, 0.7 g PVP K90 as the novel self-passivation agent and the C/N source.

As seen from Fig. 2 A insert, N-GQDs prepared under the optimal condition are bright yellow aqueous solutions under daylight irradiation (left) and show a strong blue fluorescence under UV (365 nm, right) light irradiation. The strong blue fluorescence of N-GQDs should be put down to the radiative recombination of electrons and holes resulted from nitrogen, oxygen-containing groups on N-GQDs surface [32].

As seen from Fig. 2A, the maximum fluorescence intensity occurs at 460 nm (360 nm excitation). As the excitation wavelength increasing from 355 nm to 375 nm, the maximum fluorescence spectrum peaks display red-shift, the excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence performance is attributed to the different size distributions and the different surface states formed by different functional group of N-GQDs [33]. Fig. 2B is 3D fluorescence spectrum, the fluorescence intensity, the maximum excitation peak and emission peak of N-GQDs could be directly displayed. In addition, according to Eq. (S1) (Supplementary Material), the fluorescent quantum yield of N-GQDs was calculated to be 64.2% by using quinine sulfate as standard. It was higher than the value reported in the literature [25,34,35]. The high quantum yield illustrates the as-obtained N-GQDs have excellent fluorescence properties, which indicates that the facile one-step hydrothermal synthetic route realized simultaneously the self-passivation and N-doping is reasonable and excellent for synthetizing N-GQDs.

Moreover, the stability is quite important for a fluorescent probe. Thus, the influence of ionic strength, pH, photobleaching and storage time on the fluorescent intensity of the as-obtained N-GQDs was further investigated. Fig. S3A (Supplementary Material) displays the influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs. Apparently, the fluorescent strength hardly changed when the concentrations of NaCl solution increased, manifesting the fluorescent strength of the as-obtained N-GQDs are extremely stable at different ionic strength, and it can be practically applied in salt solution. Usually, the detection is performed near neutral systems. Hence, the fluorescence intensities of the as-obtained N-GQDs were also performed under pH range from 4.0 to 8.0, and the results are displayed in Fig. S3B (Supplementary Material). As illustrated in Fig. S3B (Supplementary Material), the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs had changed less as pH values were changed from 4.0 to 8.0, indicating their fluorescence intensity kept stable at a pH value from 4.0 to 8.0. The photobleaching of the as-obtained N-GQDs was measured by detecting the fluorescent intensity seven times in 5 min and the result is shown in Fig. S3C (Supplementary Material). Evidently, the fluorescent intensity barely changed even N-GQDs was continuously bleached seven times. Moreover, the storage stability of the as-obtained N-GQDs was also measured and illustrated in Fig. S3D (Supplementary Material). It is clear that N-GQDs still revealed splendid photostability when stored at room temperature for two weeks. The aforementioned results indicated that N-GQDs exhibit perfect stability. Therefore, it was an ideal candidate material for sensing applications.

Due to the toxicity of Cr(VI) to human health, it is significant to detect Cr(VI). Thus, the detection of Cr(VI) were explored by using fluorescent probe based on N-GQDs. Fig. 3 A is fluorescence emission spectrum of N-GQDs containing various concentrations of Cr(VI), Fig. 3B is the relationship between the maximum fluorescence intensity and the logarithm (Base 10) of Cr(VI) concentration. It could be found from Fig. 3A and B that the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs decrease slowly when Cr(VI) concentration increases from 5 pM to 0.1 μM (360 nm excitation). However, they reduce quickly with Cr(VI) concentration from 0.1 to 500 μM, indicating the fluorescence turned off fleetly. As shown in Fig. 3C, an excellent linear calibration F = −18.8425lgC+6.5856 (R2 = 0.9976) of F vs. lgC was obtained when Cr(VI) concentration is from 0.1 to 100 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 91 nM (LOD = 3S
k /k, where S
k is the standard deviation, k is the slope). Compared with the previously reported values [36–43] ( Table 1
), the fluorescent probe based on N-GQDs possesses much superior sensitivity.

In order to assess whether the fluorescence of N-GQDs containing Cr(VI) was recovered, different reducing agents (Na2S2O5 and FeSO4) were adding into N-GQDs solutions containing Cr(VI). As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence strength was recovered to 86.3% and 88.6% by adding Na2S2O5 and FeSO4, respectively, compared with the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample (N-GQDs + Cr(VI) + water). Two kinds of fluorescence recovery mechanisms are discussed according to the basic principles of chemical reactions. When Na2S2O5 is added into N-GQDs solutions containing Cr(VI), the mechanism of fluorescence recovery is as follows: firstly, S2O5 2− reacts with H2O to form HSO3 − according to Eqs. (2)–(1). Since pH of N-GQDs solutions was 5.01, showing that there is a large amount of H+ in N-GQDs solutions. Then, Cr2O7 2− reacts with H+ and HSO3 − to yield Cr3+, SO4 2− and H2O according to Eqs. (2)–(2). Therefore, the results illustrate that Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr3+, the fluorescence strength of the as-obtained N-GQDs was recovered. When FeSO4 is added into N-GQDs solutions containing Cr(VI), the fluorescence recovery mechanism is as follows: Cr2O7 2− reacts with Fe2+ and H+ to produce Cr3+, Fe3+ and H2O according to Eqs. (2) and (3). The result shows that Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr3+, leading to the fluorescence recovery. In short, the mechanism of fluorescence recovery is that Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr3+ by oxidation and reduction reaction.
(2–1) S2O5 2− + H2O = 2 HSO3 −
(2-2) Cr2O7 2− + 3 HSO3 − + 5 H+ = 2 Cr3+ + 3 SO4 2− + 4H2O
(2–3) Cr2O7 2− + 6 Fe2+ + 14 H+ = 2 Cr3+ + 6 Fe3+ + 7H2O

To further evaluate the performance of the fluorescence probe based on N-GQDs, the selectivity and immunity of different metal ions and anions were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A and B, it is obviously observed that the fluorescence quenching effect was evident when Cr(VI) was added. While the other metal ions (Ag+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn(VII)) display a slight fluorescence quenching effect, the influence could be almost negligible, especially, Cr3+ is no interfere to the detection of Cr(VI). In Fig. 4C, it shows that NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, SO3 2− and CH3COO− anions almost didn’t lead to the fluorescence quenching. The results show that N-GQDs have excellent selectivity for detecting Cr(VI).

To estimate the immunity of fluorescent probe based on N-GQDs for detecting Cr(VI), water (blank sample II) and metal ions (Ag+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn(VII)) were used for controlling experiments with the experiment condition unchanged. As seen from Fig. 4D, when Cr(VI) and other metal ions (Ag+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn(VII)) coexist, the fluorescence quenching effects of metal ions on N-GQDs are similar to that of the blank sample II, indicating all the other metal ions have no effect on N-GQDs for detecting Cr(VI). The results show that N-GQDs as a fluorescent probe have excellent immunity for detecting Cr(VI).

Fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs and N-GQDs containing Cr(VI) were determined by time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC). The results are plotted in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence lifetime (τ1/τ2) of N-GQDs is 11.3042 (74.46%)/4.0926 (25.54%) ns. The total fluorescence lifetime is 9.4623 ns? The fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs containing Cr(VI) is 6.3511 ns? The results show that the fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs containing Cr(VI) is significantly decreased compared with that of N-GQDs, revealing it is a dynamic quenching process [44]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 4F shows that N-GQDs have little absorption peak at 350 nm, while the absorption peak at 350 nm for N-GQDs containing Cr(VI) is much bigger. The strengthened absorption band at 350 nm resulting from the formation of non-radiative Cr–N-GQDs strongly interfered the excitation of the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) donor [4]. Taken the above together, this fluorescence quenching mechanism is proposed as the inner filter effect (IFE), which is known to result from the absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by absorbers in the detection system [45]. Because the absorption bands (around 360 nm) of Cr(VI) fully cover the excitation bands (around 360 nm) of N-GQDs [46], most of the excitation energies (350 nm excitation) are absorbed by Cr(VI), a small amount of excitation energies (350 nm excitation) are absorbed by N-GQDs. Therefore, when Cr(VI) was added into N-GQDs solution, the fluorescence of N-GQDs was turned off.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further estimate the practical application ability of the present probe for detecting Cr(VI) in practical samples, N-GQDs was used for monitoring Cr(VI) in running water as a practical sample (running water was supplied by our own laboratory and without any treatment). Fig. 3E is fluorescence spectrum of N-GQDs in running water containing various concentrations of Cr(VI). The relationship between the maximum fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs and the logarithm (Base 10) of Cr(VI) concentration within the range of −10.3 to −3.3 in presence of tap water is shown in Fig. 3F, fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decrease slowly from −10.3 to −7.3 and reduce quickly from −7.3 to −3.3. An outstanding linear calibration correlation (coefficient was 0.9999) of F vs. lgC was acquired under Cr(VI) concentration range from 50 nM to 0.5 mM in Fig. 3F insert. The results indicate that the fluorescent probe for measuring Cr(VI) in practice sample was still sensitive. The N-GQDs fluorescent probe could be a powerful and efficient sensing system for testing Cr(VI) in practice sample.

To further assess the application of the as-obtained N-GQDs for cell bioimaging, the cytotoxicity of N-GQDs to MCF-7 cancer cells was performed through MTT assay. As illustrated in Fig. S4 (Supplementary Material), the cell viability is nearly invariable when the concentration range of the as-obtained N-GQDs was 10–100 μg mL−1, indicating that the as-obtained N-GQDs possessed low cytotoxicity, and could be considered to be an excellent bioimaging reagent.

To inspect whether N-GQDs could label the cells, MCF-7 cells were selected and cultured. Fig. 5 A is bright field image of MCF-7 cells cultured with N-GQDs for 16 h at 37 °C, the cells are uniformly dispersed, which do not show fluorescence. Fig. 5B is laser confocal microscopic image of MCF-7 cancer cells incubated with N-GQDs for 16 h at 37 °C under 405 nm excitation light. Obviously, MCF-7 cells show strong bright blue owing to the strong fluorescence from N-GQDs, illustrating that N-GQDs as a fluorescent marker successfully and efficiently entered MCF-7 cancer cells via endocytosis [47]. Consequently, N-GQDs as a fluorescent marker have the application prospects in the field of cellular imaging.

In this paper, N-GQDs with high quantum yield of 64.2% were prepared through one-step hydrothermal process using citric acid, PVP K90, glutamate as raw materials for the first time. It was of great significance to accomplish simultaneously the self-passivation and N-doping by using PVP K90 as the novel self-passivation agent. The optimal quantity of citric acid, glutamate and PVP K90 were 0.5 g, 0.5 g and 0.7 g, respectively. The as-obtained N-GQDs with a particle size approximate 1.9 ± 0.6 nm had both perfect lattice structure and twinning structure. The as-obtained N-GQDs emitted blue fluorescence at 365 nm excitation wavelengths. N-GQDs were found to be an efficient fluorescent probe for detecting Cr(VI), and it displayed a lower detection limit (91 nM) and a wider linear range (0.1–100 μM). The selectivity research demonstrated that the fluorescent probe was specific for Cr(VI). Most importantly, N-GQDs showed a good detection performance when Cr(VI) in running water was detected. Meanwhile, N-GQDs were also successfully used as MCF-7 cells marker due to the high quantum yield, excellent biocompatibility and non-toxicity. It is believed that N-GQDs as a novel fluorescence probe not only can be used for Cr(VI) detecting but also can be used for marking MCF-7 cells, showing a great potential application prospects in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51672144, 51572137, 51502149, 51702181, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2016EMB25, ZR2017PEM006, ZR2017BB013, the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

A 4 (2016) 15494-15500.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots for the sensitive detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous media and their application in test papers
